title: Cuisses de grenouille
subtitle: Frog legs sautéed in garlic butter with parsley and lemon
excerpt: Frog legs dusted in seasoned flour, sautéed quickly in foaming butter with garlic and flat-leaf parsley, finished with a squeeze of lemon. They cook in under 10 minutes and taste somewhere between very mild chicken and delicate white fish. A French brasserie classic that is simpler to make than it sounds.
sourceNotes: Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), public domain. Cuisses de grenouilles sautées method and garlic butter finishing technique. Henri-Paul Pellaprat, L'Art culinaire moderne (1935), public domain via archive.org. Frog leg preparation including flour coating and persillade garnish. Note: frog legs are not in the ingredient lookup; amounts are given in plain prose.

glossaryTerms.definition: A French mixture of finely chopped flat-leaf parsley and garlic, used as a finishing garnish for sautéed dishes. It is added to the pan off the heat (or in the last few seconds of cooking) so the parsley stays bright and the garlic does not burn.
Butter cooked until the milk solids turn golden brown and the butter smells nutty. The French name means hazelnut butter, referring to the colour and aroma. Used as a sauce and as a cooking medium.

content.text: Cuisses de grenouille are frog legs cooked in garlic butter, one of the most iconic dishes of the French brasserie. The legs are dusted in flour and sautéed quickly over high heat until golden, then finished with a
of garlic and flat-leaf parsley and a squeeze of lemon. They cook in under 10 minutes.

Frog legs are not in the ingredient lookup, so amounts are given in plain prose below. You will need about 900 g to 1 kg of frog legs for 4 people as a starter, or about 200 to 250 g per person. Frozen frog legs are widely available; thaw them fully and pat dry before cooking. Fresh legs need the same preparation.
The key is heat and speed. A large, very hot pan gives a golden crust before the legs overcook. Work in two batches if necessary to avoid steaming. The
that forms in the pan is part of the sauce; don't let it burn.
You will need approximately 900 g to 1 kg of frog legs (about 12 pairs). These are not in the ingredient lookup; amounts in the method are in plain prose.
Use a very large frying pan (30 cm or wider) to give each leg contact with the pan base. A crowded pan steams the legs rather than sautéing them.

Pat the frog legs (approximately 900 g to 1 kg, about 12 pairs) dry with kitchen paper. Season generously. Spread the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour on a plate. Dust the legs lightly in the flour and shake off the excess. Set aside on a rack while the pan heats.

Heat a large frying pan over high heat until very hot. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and let it foam. When the foam subsides and the butter begins to colour, add the frog legs in a single layer (cook in two batches if needed). Sauté for 3 to 4 minutes on each side until golden and cooked through. The legs are done when they feel firm and the flesh pulls slightly from the joint.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and stir for 30 seconds. Add the {{parsley-flat}} of flat-leaf parsley and stir again. Squeeze over the juice of {{lemon}} lemon. Toss the legs briefly in the butter and parsley. Serve immediately on warmed plates with crusty bread.

items.cause: Overcooked; frog legs cook quickly and tighten up fast
items.fix: 3 to 4 minutes per side at high heat is enough. The flesh should feel just firm, not hard

items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or too many legs at once
items.fix: Use a very hot pan and cook in batches; the pan must stay hot between legs or they will steam

items.cause: Added while the pan was still at maximum heat
items.fix: Reduce the heat before adding the garlic; add it only in the final 30 to 45 seconds of cooking

items.cause: Legs not dried before flouring, or flour applied too thickly
items.fix: Pat legs dry thoroughly before dusting; shake off all excess flour. A very light dusting gives a crisp coating; too much flour creates a paste

content.text: Frog legs have been eaten in France since at least the twelfth century, particularly in the Dombes region east of Lyon and in Burgundy, where wetland farming provided a steady supply. Escoffier lists several preparations in his guide, ranging from fritters to this simpler sautéed version with garlic and parsley. The dish is a spring and early summer speciality in its home regions, when the frogs are most active. Serve as a starter with lemon wedges and a glass of dry Alsatian Riesling.
